summary: DHH announces his return to competitive racing at the 24 Hours of Le Mans after a pandemic-era hiatus. He describes how burnout and loss of the flow state led him to consider retiring from racing after the 2020 Sebring race. After a break, watching Le Mans 2021 reignited his passion, and he secured a seat in an LMP2 car for the 2022 season. He reflects on how the track at Le Mans has always been the core draw of his racing career, and how pre-season testing confirmed the flow state was still accessible after all.
key_points: After a decade of high-level racing without pause, DHH experienced burnout and lost access to the flow state that made racing worthwhile
Sometimes the cure for burnout is simply a break — watching Le Mans 2021 from the outside reignited his desire to compete
Le Mans holds a singular place in his racing life, with close to a thousand laps over the years and an undiminished pull
Pre-season testing initially felt like a mistake, but the brain quickly acclimatized and the flow state returned within hours
He returns with a strong record (class win in 2014, four podiums in eight appearances) but frames 2022 as having nothing to prove — which paradoxically fuels the competitive drive
money_quotes: I actually thought I was going to hang up the helmet after the pandemic.
That the portal to the pure state of flow had been closed. That shutting the cockpit door no longer kept the world out and the focus in.
But sometimes all you need is a break.
It may sound glamorous, but the reality is that you rarely get to see much outside the track when traveling for racing.
After close to a thousand laps over the years, it still pulls like nothing else.
There's nothing to prove. Which of course means we'll be throwing everything at it to win!
After the first ten laps, I thought, shit, I've made a mistake. I've totally lost the feel. But then snap, the brain acclimates.
The world had once again been completely isolated to outside the cockpit, the flow state was constant, and the smile couldn't be wider.
key_insight: Burnout from a passionate pursuit doesn't mean the passion is gone — sometimes all it takes is a real break to rediscover the flow state that made it worthwhile.
